summary: Paul Graham argues that YC was never a one-man show and that Jessica Livingston’s character judgment and culture-building were central to YC’s early success. He claims YC’s defining advantage was selecting founders well, and that Jessica’s “social radar” shaped the alumni network and the organization’s values. Her reluctance to seek attention, combined with public narratives, has obscured her role despite her outsized impact.
key_points: YC’s distinctive culture and founder selection were primarily shaped by Jessica Livingston.
Authenticity and character assessment were critical to YC’s success, especially at the earliest stages.
Avoiding bad actors strengthened the YC network and long-term value.
Jessica’s aversion to attention and public conflict led to her contributions being underrecognized.
Her insights about founders are among the most consequential for startup outcomes and economic growth.
money_quotes: If you don't understand her, you don't understand YC.
At YC, the culture was the product.
Authenticity is one of the most important things YC looks for in founders.
We thought Airbnb was a bad idea. We funded it because we liked the founders.
The person who knows the most about the most important factor in the growth of mature economies — that is who Jessica Livingston is.
key_insight: YC’s success hinged less on prescience about technology and more on Jessica Livingston’s ability to read founders and build a culture of authenticity.
